video_title: Samsung Galaxy S26 Review | Small Phone, Big Problems?
text: You should. I write messy but Imma give you a no filter review, no in-depth profound stuff, just what I noticed raw, after getting this phone from pre-order, early, and coming from a mid-range, the switch was so underwhelming. If I should list the changes I noticed... the only changes I'd say is the smoothness, the smooth animation (compared to a mid-range phone), and the new AI capabilities, kinda disappointed though by their eraser, in editing, as they removed the normal eraser and combined it with their AI eraser so removing a very little thing will give you a watermark that says the photo is AI generated or something, regardless of how little you edited. While the smoothness of it, is noticeable, it's not a change I'm so suprised and wow'ed by. The camera too is soooo underwhelming, the fact that I got it the same price as the Flip 6 (w/ discount), and S26 has a worst camera, it's so... meh, disappointing, whilst the flip has a way better camera quality, the Samsung mid-range (had an A55) it's so underwhelming it feels like it has the same camera quality as the S26 base model, I don't see much big difference if you're just gonna look at it raw but if you tested it in detail there will be noticeable difference, until with the Flip, the differentlce is huge and easy to notice, so I'm not gagging that hard, for this price difference? Nope, boring. The only difference is the processing of details when you zoom in, the S26 is way better, but honestly not life changing, way better but like I said despite the difference it's not something I'm gagging over. But the S26 do got the vertical lock, the only good change I noticed, the base doesn't have the privacy screen feature, obviously. Only the ultra got it. In conclusion it's very underwhelming for me. I actually thought of buying iPhone 17 when I was waiting and contemplating before the S26 release, but as someone who's been mainly using Samsung for almost a decade now, I'm very used to its feature and there's no way that I'll be switching and paying for a phone like an iPhone that lacks lots of things that makes using my phone easy for me, I can't pay that much and be more disappointed than being underwhelmed, despite liking iPhone's premium feels and stuff. And I'd say for me the base iPhone and Pixel has a more noticable better camera quality than the S26, idk their specs on paper but I prefer how clear Pixel and iPhone 17's camera is, base on hands on exprience. But would I still choose S26 if I knew that before hand? Probably yeah, as someone who came from another Samsung device, I'm used to it, and I feel in love with the light weight of it, and small size, like I said even if the other flag ship base models of Apple and Google are the same size (cuz I want a smaller phone) Samsung has features for me that makes it easy and smarter to use, puls the new and better Galaxy AI capabilities, I like it, as someone who utilize with those assistance fetaures a lot. Not to disregard those new features, but overall for me, the switch is still underwhelming, not totally disappointed, not bad, not at all, but I'd say it's real just underwhelming. But I feel like if you go for the Ultra that will give a better noticeable change. But if your older phone is still doing well, and you're fine with that don't bother, wait for a real upgrade. (I kinda hope they have a phone that physically feels like the S26 base model (the size, the weight) while having an S Ultra features, I would have gone for that.)

video_title: The Problem with the Galaxy S26
text: Dropping titanium from the newest Galaxy S26 line was actually a very good thing. Titanium is heavier than aluminum. It also retains heat, which is a problem you don't want in a phone. Using aluminum allows the phone to cool itself more efficiently. Titanium should never have been used on a phone. Samsung isn't the only one that dropped the titanium. Apple was the first to get rid of it last year. And yes, the S26 base model is no longer considered a true flagship phone from Samsung. All phone manufacturers will push you to the largest and most advanced variant. Again, Apple does this with the iPhone 17 Pro Max. And when it comes to the Galaxy S26 Ultra specifically, it matches or exceeds every Galaxy foldable spec wise. The only thing it doesn't do is fold. Which, in my opinion, is a very good thing. I would rather have a slab phone that will last me a long time than a foldable that will break if it isn't handled with kid's gloves. The base S26 is the entry point into the S line. Samsung and other manufacturers will always hold back a feature or two to entice you to upgrade. Something Apple is the king of when it comes to their computers for example when it comes to storage or RAM. Almost any manufacturer will dangle a carrot in front of you to get you to upgrade.

video_title: The Problem with the Galaxy S26
text: Strong disagree. It is easy to compare S25 vs S26, or even S22 vs S26 and say "Samsung is slacking", but please, let's blame Samsung after analyzing the competition once. iPhone 17 - 120Hz! Finally! YAY! But still uses USB 2.0, still charges at 25 Watts, still has a dual-camera setup. Hardware-wise, that is still NOT par with the Galaxy S20 from 2020, which had 120Hz, USB 3.0, 45-Watt charging and a 3-camera setup with 8K ability! Pixel 10 is far more competitive - 120Hz, USB 3.0, faster 30W charging, and the brand new 5x Optical Zoom lens. This does bring some competition to Samsung, but the Tenor G5 is still somewhat behind the Snapdragon 8 Gen 2 from Galaxy S23 series! So even this allows Samsung to still be a strong contender without doing any real work. So yeah, complain all you want about the S26 being "boring" but Samsung has no reason to make any improvements when the competition is struggling to out-do what they did in 2020. Also do ask yourself - Why was the world singing praises for the iPhone 17, if the Samsung is underwhelming according to you? Just the Apple effect?

text: ​@w45dwhen it comes to battery drain I would say it's slightly improved over the S25 but you won't notice it until you look for the difference. video_title: Galaxy S26 Review - What's Wrong with it?
text: This is in relation to Privacy display masking lost in Notification panel Device : Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra Settings: Turn on Privacy display - OFF Condition for turning on - ON Maximum privacy protection - ON (Tested with Google Calendar app). ​The Issue: Privacy Display correctly masks incoming notification pop-ups. However, if the pop-up times out without being touched and moves to the notification panel, the masking is lost. Swiping down the notification panel later reveals the full, unmasked content. ​Expected Behavior: The notification should remain masked in the notification panel until actively tapped by the user. Actual Behavior: The notification is completely unmasked in the notification panel, exposing private data to anyone nearby (e.g., while commuting in a bus, train etc.).

text: ​@batatafritacortesit's partly true at least. I knew nothing about cameras, but I've finally learned some things and through playing around with settings I now actually take photos with my s25 base model that I'm like 'woah this is actually a really good photo.' One amazing thing you can do on samsung s25 if you can create a "custom filter". Sounds complex but it's dead simple. What do you like most about iphone photos? Say you like your skin tone better on iphone photos. Then go get a photo from an iphone 17 pro max of you and download it on your samsung. Next, open "filters" and click on + to create a custom filter. Now click on the iphone photo. Next, go to settings and click "filters" on "settings to keep." Now every time you open your camera it will use this "iphone filter" by default where it copies to the best of it's ability the colour tones, mood etc of the iphone photo you love. That's one way to do it. There are other even better ways, like using custom settings in Pro Mode that use the same settings that you like about iphone pics (for example, iphone tends to use higher "temperature" which adds a bit more yellow/orange whereas samsung tends to do the opposite - add a more blue cool tone. You would have to play around and learn but trust me if you finally learn how to make basic filters or default settings on things like temperature (blue vs yellow) or tint (green versus magenta) and contrast, you will finally get photos that look BETTER than iphone because they will be to your exact taste. It takes time. But worth it

video_title: WHAT'S WRONG with the S26 Ultra?
text: ​@ShrekVapeReviewsLet's start off with Samsung's success, the Note series. 2011-2020 Note era: Samsung became popular because of their Note series. Unlike other phones, you had S Pen support, a bigger display, and many more features like a better camera, better battery life, and many more exclusive Note features, which grabbed people's attention. Other experimental series like the Galaxy Beam, which was their projector phone, and the Galaxy Z4 Zoom or Galaxy Edge series didn't last long, whereas the Note lived for 10 years and stood out from other brands. No brands ever came close to the Galaxy Note, let alone Galaxy S phones in the early era. Over time, the difference became smaller, and the Galaxy Note series was a Galaxy S series phone with an S Pen, which made the lineup confusing and complicated, and I was hoping for a merge. Then came the S21 Ultra. Samsung faced significant backlash for removing the S Pen slot altogether because they didn't want to make their Note 20 obsolete, which was an understatement as this was a 6-month-old phone and too early to make obsolete. Samsung faced sales degradation, and many loyal Samsung fans switched to other brands. The temporary S22-S24 Ultra reincarnation was to win back most of the loyal Note fans and to keep them in confidence, and they won back some users, but most loyal users had already adjusted to another brand. From the Note 20 Ultra - S22 Ultra era, S Pen usage decreased from the Note era. S Pen transition phase: Unlike the S21 Ultra, which jumped straight to S Pen slot abandonment and faced significant backlash, they are training us by making the S Pen less and less useful and introducing Galaxy AI features like AI Select and Creative Studio, making the S Pen a toy and rounding the corners with the S25 Ultra & S26 Ultra, taking further steps towards this approach, which will make a lot of people forget their phone has an S Pen. While easing the loyal Note fans who are attached to the S Pen, Samsung is slowly trying to detach Note fans and training them to migrate to traditional S phones. If they removed the slot with the S25 Ultra & S26 Ultra, they would have to reincarnate the Note series again. Instead, they are training users, and full abandonment altogether would be a huge loss for Samsung, and they are trying to avoid significant S21 Ultra backlash by not mentioning the S Pen in their event for the past two generations of Ultra. Baby steps: It really doesn't make sense for them to bring back Bluetooth S Pen, as many speculated with the S26 Ultra, if they are getting ready to abandon the S Pen slot with the S27 Ultra, although samsung hasn't confirmed this but reliable leakers have told us since s25 launch and Samsung is showing us signs by making the corner more round and pushing the S Pen to its absolute edge tells us it's almost gone anyways, where the S27 Ultra will transition to the S21 Ultra phase where Samsung has an excuse that it takes up 10-15% room which prevent 6000mAh battery and this whole slim and light weight hype as samsung is trying to train users toward a thin phone as the s25 edge got backlash who asked for this so now they are slowly slimming phones down and looking for another excuse slim hype and bigger battery which is needed, where their data base spen usage too low, demand for bigger battery much higher, its called business my friend. Maybe the S28 Ultra will carry that legacy. Then, by the S29 Ultra or S30 Ultra, they will likely abandon the S Pen entirely because they will have a strong database that less than 1% of people bought the S Pen, like they did with the S21 Ultra, and from Samsung training us, they won't listen this time. So, That's the goal, my friend: to make the S Pen less relevant before removal, not repeating the S21 Ultra mistake.

video_title: Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra Review: There's a Catch
text: You hit the nail on the head! You're 100% correct—the Privacy Display on the Galaxy S26 Ultra is a specialized piece of hardware, not just a software trick. Because it’s BUILT INTO the PHYSICAL SCREEN at the pixel level, an app from the PLAY STORE, & or GALAXY STORE can't truly REPLICATE how it works. Here’s why your question about those apps is actually a great one to dig into: ​1. HARDWARE vs SOFTWARE "Privacy" Vs "NYB" Not Your Business ​The Hardware (S26 Ultra): It actually uses two different types of pixels (narrow and wide). When you turn on privacy mode, it physically shuts off or dims the pixels that spread light to the sides, leaving only the "straight-forward" pixels active. It’s like a physical set of blinds inside your screen that you can open and close. ​The Apps (Software): Any app you find on the store is just a software overlay. These apps usually just put a dark, semi-transparent filter over your screen or create a "grid" pattern to make it harder to read from a distance. They don't actually change the direction of the light, so someone sitting right next to you can still see your screen—it'll just look like you have your brightness turned way down. ​2. Is it still a good question to ask? ​Actually, yes, and here’s why: ​For Older Devices: Since you mentioned your other Samsung tech, those devices don't have this "Flex Magic Pixel" hardware. For those, a software app or a physical "stick-on" privacy protector is the only way to get a similar effect. ​ Comparison: It’s worth asking how those apps compare to the built-in tech. REVIEWERS like MKBHD, & MRWHO'STHEBOSS. have pointed out that the built-in hardware is "JAMES BOND level" because it doesn't ruin your screen QUALITY 24/7 like a cheap app or a permanent plastic screen protector does. ​The Bottom Line: You're right that an app can't "give" a phone the S26 Ultra's hardware feature. If a phone doesn't have those special PIXELS, no app in the world can make the light behave that way. Those apps are basically just "DIGITAL SUNGLASSES" for your phone, whereas the S26 Ultra has actual "PRIVACY SHUTTERS" BUILT into the GLASS. From GEMINI.

video_title: Samsung Galaxy S26 Ultra vs iPhone 17 Pro Max vs Galaxy S25 Ultra - Battery Drain Test
text: This is an outstanding and, above all, realistic 10-hour battery drain test! It’s impressive to see the Samsung S26 Ultra staying within a tiny 3-minute margin of the iPhone 17 Pro Max, despite stuck with the proven 5,000 mAh capacity. This proves how significantly Samsung has optimized the efficiency of the processor and display – and that’s even with the Privacy Display enabled!. Let’s be real: calling a 3-minute difference a 'lag' ignores the actual user experience. Much more exciting is the boot speed: seeing the direct comparison, the iPhone feels like a snail starting up compared to the S26 Ultra. That’s the true power of the Snapdragon 8 Elite and the optimized UFS storage. However, what’s missing in this test (and almost all others) is a re-test after 14 days of usage. During the first few days, the Galaxy AI works overtime in the background to analyze user behavior and index data. Only after this learning phase does 'Adaptive Battery' fully kick in to proactively manage background drain. I’d bet that in a two-week re-test, that 3-minute gap doesn't just disappear – the S26 Ultra likely pulls ahead of the iPhone thanks to this intelligent background hygiene. I’m also quite critical of the comparison with the Honor Magic 8 Pro. Sure, 6,270 mAh sounds great on a spec sheet, but what about software sovereignty in 5 years?. Who guarantees that Honor users will still get Android updates in 2031 or won’t be forced into a different OS due to sanctions?. Samsung guarantees 7 years of updates and monthly security patches. To me, true sustainability means a device physically designed for cell protection (60W instead of 100W+ stress) and a software fortress that stays secure until 2033. This background activity typically leads to higher thermal output and accelerated battery drain. Since the S25 Ultra in this test is already fully optimised and 'settled', it holds a distinct advantage in efficiency and stability. A re-test in two weeks would likely yield more accurate results once the S26 Ultra’s background processes have stabilised.

text: Being completely honest as a Samsung fan, most of the negative sentiment around the S26 Ultra comes down to one thing — the price. And frankly, that criticism is entirely justified. When you're being asked to spend €1,600 on a smartphone, you lose the right to ask consumers to overlook any compromises. At that price point, nitpicking isn't entitlement, it's a reasonable expectation. Yes, as a fans we can look past certain shortcomings and still appreciate what the phone offers — but not the average consumer walking into a store and handing over €1,600. That person believes and deserves the absolute best in every single category, no asterisks, no trade-offs. And unfortunately, Samsung seems to have adopted Apple's playbook: introduce a headline feature, quietly cut corners somewhere else to offset the cost. It's a pattern that's becoming harder to ignore, and at this price tier, harder to forgive. If Samsung wants to charge a premium, they need to deliver without compromise — full stop unless they want to be another Apple.

However, if the pop-up times out without being touched and moves to the notification panel, the masking is lost. Swiping down the notification panel later reveals the full, unmasked content. ​Expected Behavior: The notification should remain masked in the notification panel until actively tapped by the user. video_title: The Pixel 10 Has a Performance Problem.
text: The anticipated performance increase from a new driver update for the Pixel 10's GPU, which is currently stuck around 396 \text{ MHz}, is potentially massive. The general expectation in the community and from tech experts is that a proper driver update will unlock the GPU's true clock speed and address the current performance deficit. Here is a breakdown of the potential increase: * Expected Clock Speed: The PowerVR DXT-48-1536 GPU is officially rated with a peak clock speed of around 1.0 \text{ GHz} to 1.1 \text{ GHz}. * The Multiplier: If the driver fixes the issue and allows the GPU to boost to its intended maximum clock speed (1.1 \text{ GHz}), that is nearly a 3-fold increase in frequency over the current 396 \text{ MHz} cap. Expected Performance Boost Based on Google's history of fixing GPU performance with driver updates on previous Pixel devices (which saw 30\% to 62\% increases) and the current severe throttling: * In Benchmarks: Current reports show the Pixel 10 Pro scoring a mere 3,707 in Geekbench's GPU test, which is significantly lower than the Pixel 9 Pro's score of 9,023. A fix is expected to close this gap and likely place the performance well above the Pixel 9 Pro. * Expert Estimates: Some experts predict a performance uplift of 30\% or more on the new chip. Considering the clock speed difference, the performance increase in synthetic benchmarks that heavily utilize the GPU could be even more dramatic if the GPU runs consistently closer to its intended 1.1 \text{ GHz} peak. * Real-World Impact: You can expect significant improvements in: * High-end Gaming: Smoother frame rates, ability to use higher graphics settings, and better sustained performance. * Thermal and Battery Life: A better-optimized driver will allow the GPU to finish tasks faster (race-to-idle) and operate more efficiently, which often leads to less heat generation and improved battery life, even at higher clock speeds. In summary, the new driver is expected to unlock a GPU running at roughly 36\% of its potential, leading to a massive jump in graphics performance, likely placing it comfortably above its predecessor, the Pixel 9.

video_title: Google Pixel 10: The Biggest Camera Letdown of 2025!
text: There is some discussion suggesting the Google Pixel 10 might be seen as a downgrade compared to the Pixel 9, particularly concerning its camera hardware. However, it's essential to remember that Pixel phones rely primarily on their advanced AI capabilities and computational photography, rather than just the specific sensors they use. When compared to an older model, the Pixel 10 might lag slightly in night photography. Despite this, in no way can one say the final result is worse or truly a 'downgrade,' as the AI processing is a major factor in the final image quality. Furthermore, the Pixel 10 features significant upgrades in other areas. It includes a 10.8MP telephoto camera with 5x optical zoom, a better processor compared to the previous model, faster charging, and a larger battery capacity. Moreover, when compared to a device like the Xiaomi 14T PRO (I had been using xiaomi phones since Xiaomi 12), the overall user experience regarding software, photos, and AI capabilities is significantly different. These differences are substantial enough to make choosing the Google Pixel 10 phone the clear decision. On the negative side, I would add the limited storage capacities available (128/256 GB) and its high price.

text: My Pixel 10 Pro has worse battery life than my 2.5 year old OP11. One key factor of consideration is that I changed cell carriers at the same time I replaced my phone. I went from Metro by Tmobile to Visible by Verizon. This could be contributing to an unfair comparison, as the Verizon coverage doesn't seem to great here in LA area. When the Pixel is on wifi, it's passive battery drain is about half of cellular. I've turned off 5g through the dialer codes and the cellular drain isn't quite as much now. Last night, on wifi, the phone drained about 13% passively while sitting on the night stand. That still seems quite high. I recall my OP11 only draining a few percent overnight, but I don't really remember. The battery life was so good the whole time I owned the OP11 that I never really paid attention and analyzed these things. I do not game on my phone, light usage of calls, messages, and youtube mainly. A bit of fitbit app and Gemini as well. I haven't been on social media at all. At it's current performance, I get a little battery anxiety. Not bad since chargers are all around us, but still.

text: Imagine you have a picture on your computer. When you "digitally zoom" in, you're just enlarging the pixels in that picture. The image gets bigger, but it also gets blurry and you lose quality, just like when you zoom in on a photo you've already taken on your phone. Optical zoom is completely different. Think of it like a pair of binoculars. When you use binoculars, they have a special lens that physically brings the far-away object closer to your eyes before the image is even captured. So, when a phone like the Pixel 10 has a dedicated 5x optical zoom camera, it's like a built-in pair of binoculars. It has a separate lens specifically for that 5x zoom, so it's a completely new, high-quality photo taken at that distance. It's not just a digital crop of the main camera, which is what most phones do when you zoom past their optical limit. That's why the quality is so much better and clearer—it's using real lenses to get closer, not just software.

video_title: The Pixel 10 Has a Performance Problem.
text: We need the correct new GPU driver, the phone is being held back. The Pixel 10 shipped with a GPU driver (v24.3) that was already out of date at the time of the phone’s release. Imagination Technologies, the company that makes the PowerVR GPU in the Pixel 10 range, rolled out a newer driver (v25.1) back in August. That updated driver specifically added Android 16 compatibility (that's right, the Android OS the phone was released with isn't even properly supported by the GPU yet), Vulkan 1.4 support, and other broader improvements. That should make the gameplay quality, something people have complained about a lot, a lot better. It's absolutely crazy that Google didn't release the phone with the correct driver when they must've known it would have the most scrutiny at launch, much less channels are going to want to go back after a driver improvement to do another review, they'll have moved on to the "next thing".

text: Wow. Every time I see a reply like this, I'm reminded how little anyone knows about the inside of this business. If I were as captured as you claim, that *entire* AI section would have been praise, instead of the extended criticism about LLMs/SLMs it actually was. Magic Cue is a major tentpole of the new Pixel line, and essentially all I did here was document how it doesn't work (while praising older features that are less in line with Google's current marketing focus, like At A Glance). These comments also always ignore the fact that I routinely criticize brands that fly me to California, Europe, Korea and beyond - often while sitting in the very hotels they're paying for - and I've never even so much as been threatened to change my tune because of that travel/lodging reimbursement (which I disclose every time in the name of transparency, not ... whatever you're implying). As for the camera: samples speak for themselves. They're all over the video, including the low light shots that are apparently so awful. Almost like there's more to a device than the specs. Meanwhile: you fail to mention Google adding a 5x telephoto to their base model, a BOM cost increase that surely informed its decision to downgrade the other sensors on an entry-tier device, knowing camera nerds will gravitate toward the Pro. Cherry picking: gotta love it. I've watched some of my older Pocketnow stuff recently too. It was a great time, when there was a lot more to criticize on hardware because that's where all the action was. Today, it's mostly a software story - which I criticize a lot in this very video, if you're willing to actually listen.

text: Portrait model, telephoto use cheyathinte Gemini prompt cheytha output - Why Is Google Doing This? (The Theories) Users and reviewers have a few theories, none of them confirmed by Google: Software Legacy: Google's entire portrait algorithm was built around using dual-pixel data from a single wide-angle sensor (back from the Pixel 2). Users speculate that Google has simply refused to or has been too lazy to re-engineer its entire portrait pipeline to incorporate data from two separate lenses (the main + telephoto) for depth mapping. Hardware Quality: This is a strong theory for the base model Pixel 10. As we discussed, the telephoto lens on the base 10 is a smaller, lower-quality 10.8MP sensor. It's possible this sensor doesn't perform well in anything but perfect lighting, so Google forces the camera to use the superior (but downgraded) 48MP main sensor for all portrait processing, as it can gather more light and detail. Pro Model Differentiation (Which Failed): The baffling part is that even the Pixel 10 Pro (with its high-quality 48MP telephoto) also doesn't use the 5x lens for portrait mode, which debunks the "Pro" differentiation theory and points back to a core software/philosophy problem. The "Workaround" (And Why It's Not Good Enough) Users have found a workaround, but it's clunky and highlights the problem: Switch to the regular "Photo" mode. Manually select the 5x telephoto lens. Take a regular, non-portrait photo. Go into Google Photos, find the picture, tap "Edit," and use the "Portrait blur" tool to artificially add the blur after the fact. While the server-side AI for this tool is very good, users are annoyed that this is an extra, multi-step process instead of a simple, real-time feature in the main camera app.

You have to manually invoke it, and if you're connected to Android Auto, it doesn't work at all because it doesn't appear as an option when a call comes in. The Pixel also has the absolute best voice-to-text of any phone, hands down. Yes, you can get it on other Android phones with GBoard, but it's demonstrably slower and you can edit with your voice like you can on the Pixel. And I don't know what you're doing to cause "laggy animations and dropped frames" (maybe it's all the stuff you have to do to achieve your customizations), but my 10 Pro XL is absolutely buttery smooth. It's just as smooth as my iPhone 17 Pro and way more smooth than the S25 Ultra I just sold.

text: @whyhaveaname5963 You're trying a little too hard to undersell it. It's a 48mp camera (vs the Pixel's 12.5), so you are already getting more detail at 4X and then it can crop the sensor to 8x and still achieve optical quality. That's one of the best telephoto setups since the Galaxy S24 with 10x. As far as what else they are doing: Larger, longer battery life, more powerful processor, a pretty significant front-facing camera that does both normal and wide-screen shots in either portrait or landscape. Pixel went hard on software tricks (half of which aren't even working well) and iPhone went hard on hardware changes. But keep coping.

video_title: Google Pixel 10 Pro full review
text: Google’s Tensor AI chip is the smartest move they’ve made since launching Pixel. For years, Apple’s dominance came from building their own silicon, but now Google is playing the same game and hitting harder in the area that matters most: AI-driven user experience. Real-time translation, call screening, and creative tools are exactly where Apple can’t easily compete, and as a user I’m already seeing the payoff. But here’s the insult, I paid $1,500 (tax included) for this device, and what do I get in the box? A USB-C cable. That’s it. No charging brick, no earphones, no extras to justify the premium. For this kind of price tag, the box should come with at least the basics or even a nice case or accessory to reward loyal Pixel owners. Google has fired a bullet at Apple with Tensor, but by copying Apple’s worst habit selling less in the box for more money they risk souring the very audience that’s cheering them on. Brilliant strategy, disappointing execution at the checkout.

video_title: Xiaomi 15 Long Term Review - 6 Months Later! Flawed but STILL THE BEST COMPACT FLAGHSIP🔥
text: Colors are a choice. Hate it or love it. I have compared it against OnePlus 13 and you cannot separate the two, both having pros and cons. On the flip side, Gcam community is strong with Xiaomi, and my Gcam config will literally nullify all your "color science" issues. And I do like the color science when shooting nature and cityscapes. It is a unique look, an acquired taste, no complaints with detail retention which is always strong. Regarding telephoto, you are forgetting it's a 6.3" form factor phone sharing sensor space with a way bigger 1/1.3" sensor with f1.6 aperture (other similar sized phones packing 1/1.56" sensor with smaller lens apertures as well) and despite that the telephoto is top notch, with f2.0 aperture and in my indepth test against X200 FE which has a 1/1.95" sensor (but with f2.7 aperture), the Xiaomi 15 was equal/better during the day but better at night. Check on my channel, it's almost a 1 hr comparison video, with pixel peeping.. I would say the OP13 has a slightly better tele while being a gigantic phone. 60mm f2.0 focal length is very suitable for street photography. I hate Xiaomi 17 Pro's 115mm focal length without including a short tele.

video_title: Xiaomi 15 Long Term Review - 6 Months Later! Flawed but STILL THE BEST COMPACT FLAGHSIP🔥
text: Very good detailed review but can't agree on the cameras being top 3. Colours are quite off in many circumstances in photos and the daytime video quality is oversaturated, way too high in contrast and oversharpened. You have pointed out the contrasty video. I think the video quality on the Xiaomi 17 is a bit better. Some of Xiaomi's previous phones also had better video quality than the Xiaomi 15. The Xiaomi 13 for example in daylight produced much more natural video quality. The contrast at least is a bit lower. The other drawback of the cameras is the jn5 telephoto. The focal range is too short. It's probably the only major Chinese brand in the price category that doesn't have a periscope and zoom performance is behind basically every Chinese brand's standard flagship.

video_title: Xiaomi 15 Long Term Review - 6 Months Later! Flawed but STILL THE BEST COMPACT FLAGHSIP🔥
text: The phone itself is good, I had it and returned after a few days. The biggest thing, that I didn't like in it is that it does not allow me to set a reduction of blue light as I want. It reduces it slighly but not that good as other brands do. I could not use it at night because the amount of blue light was still high for me. Another thing is the camera. It does a great job, but if you have a child, every 3 photo is usuable and the rest are blury, even with enabled action mode. Maybe I did something wrong, because according to other opinion in this topic, the phone does a great job if you have a children or animals, but mine did not. If you take a photos or non movable objects, then you will be more than happy. Even at night the X15 takes a great shoots. I do not understand why does Xiaomi keep X2.6 as a telephoto lens, when many phones have already X5. The next thing is heat. It is not hot, and I am not a gamer, but the phone is constantly warm when you browse Internet or do simple task. You can feel it even if it fits a case. I am not a fan of this feeling. The last thing, but not least, is the HyperOS in global version. It seems like, it is more like HyperOS lite in comparision to CN version, because there are some stuff that are missing, you do not even have a usable widget for a calendar. HyperOS is also not that fluid as you probably believe. ColorOS or even OriginOS are in this case better. So at the end I just gave up and ordered a Vivo X300.

video_title: Xiaomi 15 - The ULTIMATE Camera Review!
text: ​@dracielbwhorogium5728 I also got it but decided to return it. The telephoto is the worst in its segment compared to its Chinese competitors. it's a small 1/2.75" jn5 sensor and is not a periscope with only a 60mm upright telephoto. It's competitors at the same price or cheaper (OPPO find x8, Vivo x200, Oneplus 13, Realme gt7 pro) are all using a much better Sony 1/1.95" periscope sensor at at least 70mm. There is no comparison. I also found the video quality poor with excessive contrast and sharpening. Unfortunately the Xiaomi 17 is making no improvement to the already weakest telephoto in the segment (at least in the Chinese market). I agree with the reviewer here. The strength of the camera is lowlight but i found daylight pics very inconsistent and i especially found videos very disappointing

video_title: Xiaomi 15 Ultra FULL REVIEW - The New Camera King!? 👑
text: Really good phone, but... The price on the EU market is higher than the original Chinese price by more than €500. Why? No market reasons can justify such a difference. The price of the 1TB model in China is lower than the EU price for 512GB. In the EU (at least for now) there is no 1TB model, only 512GB for €1499. Out of 4 color variants, we get only two: black (with glass back) and chrome silver (with plastic back). Are they trying to sell us a plastic phone for €1499? For God's sake! Furthermore, in my local EU Xiaomi webshop during the promotional period, you get... Xiaomi's Electric Scooter 4 Lite as a gift?! Some other markets get much more useful and appropriate promotional gifts, e.g. Xiaomi Pad 7 Pro 8GB+256GB, Xiaomi 120W HyperCharge Combo, Xiaomi 15 Ultra Photography Kit (only logical!), additional discount, etc. But, I guess they had to get rid of the stock of those scooters, so someone must get them as a gift. As pointless as it may be. The battery of the original (and €500 cheaper) Chinese variant is 6000mAh. The battery for the EU has been downgraded to 5410 mAh. We don't have a charger in the box again. Why do they put a smaller battery for the EU? Because they can. Even with such a degraded battery, it will probably last longer and charge faster than the competition on the EU market. In the domestic Chinese market, their competition is stronger, so they try harder. By the way, the EU Common Charger Directive 2022/2380 requires manufacturers to ensure the possibility of purchasing a product without a charger, which means that manufacturers should offer a product with and without a charger, not just without a charger. The EU does not require the sale of mobile phones without a charger, or with batteries of lower strength, these are manufacturers' fairy tales. And greed. So in short: if I bought this, I would pay 500€ more for a product with a degraded battery, no charger, and a useless promo-gift. As a long-time Xiaomi user, I have only one thing to say to them: So long!

text: Right, let's clear this up. No, I most certainly did not say that. I'm rather perplexed by your interpretation, as that wasn't the point of my video at all. To reiterate the key arguments: 1. The Xiaomi 15 Ultra is the first smartphone since the iPhone 15 Pro to genuinely excite me. 2. Xiaomi are marketing this device as a powerhouse for filmmakers, as evidenced by their official presentations and promotional materials. 3. However, they've fallen short in delivering on that promise for three specific reasons. 4. These issues are easily rectifiable with sufficient feedback from filmmakers who are interested in the device. 5. In the meantime, the phone can still achieve professional-grade results, particularly with RAW video and ProRes, when used with a third-party application. It's all rather straightforward, and I'm struggling to understand where you're finding confusion with the video's central thesis. Regarding your incorrect assumption that I "believe Xiaomi's target audience is exclusively professional videographers and photographers, LOL": That would be far too niche a market for any smartphone manufacturer to target solely. RED Digital Cinema attempted this with the RED Hydrogen One in 2018, and it was a commercial failure. However, what is undeniable is that smartphone manufacturers are increasingly emphasising the professional capabilities of their camera systems for both photography and videography, and promoting them accordingly. Furthermore, these devices are being used more and more by professionals. The iPhone, in particular, has seen significant adoption (with directors such as Zack Snyder, Steven Soderbergh, and Sean Baker using it), but as I demonstrated in the video, the Xiaomi 15 Ultra has the potential to surpass the iPhone. Features such as ProRes Apple Log on the iPhone are not aimed at the average consumer; they are clearly professional tools. Similarly, ACES Log on the 15 Ultra is a specialised professional feature, at least in its theoretical application. This, coupled with 10-bit capture and full manual controls in the native Xiaomi camera app's 'pro' video mode, strongly indicates that these devices are designed to appeal to professionals, not just the everyday consumer.

text: Mirror has nothing to do with lenses quality topic. Modern lenses are in fact slighty better but due to newly projected optic, not due to mirror removing. Mirror has other very significant Advantages, so for me mirroless models ale literally worthless. They are unfortunately huge downgrade for me to date. So I'm forced to privately still using last produced dslrs like Canon 7DmkII or 5D mkIV. But on daily basis, I'm using 14 ultra instead. 15 Ultra is also far worse than 14 ultra. 15 is not upgrade, it's unacceptable huge downgrade. There is no AI forced in mobile photography, you have full direct control on each aspect of pure jpg, also what is most important you have full control over sharpening and saturation. AI is used only for tele in case of in overall very bad quality auto mode. Auto mode should never be used due to lack of control over terrible processing, even still in xiaomi. Sharpening and saturation must be controlled intentionally and manually. Xiaomi and vivo are allowing that. Pure raw never should be used, only scenarios for pure raw are sensor tests. Instead, ultra raw format can be used - with data from multiplication. Which are creating wide dynamic range, better details and less Bayers interpolation artifacts. But, when you have control over jpg processing, even ultra raw is not needed if you are not planning extended post editing. Ex/pro raw must be used in samsung and apple phones, where is no control over jpg. Jpg in samsung and apple is entairly worthless and extremally bad. Raw itself is presented without sharpening and processing so can be perceived as loosing quality, but this is not true. In fact details and other aspects are far better than in case of processed auto. Can be processed further intentionally in controlled way. But, used demosaic algorithm is important. Not every algorithm is able to properly work with xiaomi raw. Ultra raw due to better included data in such cases has less artifacts. Raw does not have any AI and any processing, even does not have multiplication, but last one is con. Multiplication is physically adding technical value and it's main advantage above dslr. Ultra raw (format not possible for dslr) is raw plus multiplication, but still without AI and processing. Ultra raw is far better. But (almost) not needed (white balance and color gamut, bit depth or compression level sometimes require raw in case of deep photo editing), when jpg can be fully controlled. All object texures when proper demosaic algorithm is used are 1:1 with apsc with good lens, with better dynamic range and slighty better resolution (in case of jpg or ultra raw, pure raw has less data, far worse dynamic range and more details artifacts).

video_title: Xiaomi 15 Ultra: Why Your Smartphone Camera Isn’t As Good As You Think…
text: I have both the 15U and Pixel 9 Pro as my secondary. And the photo differences is night and day. XM's system looks closer to my RX100, while the Pixel is what one would call a "processed image" on steroids. For the sake of HDR, Pixel does a good job, but I think it's too mainstream and artificial (now) to flatten out highlights and shadows. Plus, I couldn't stand the noisy low light/zoom images on the Pixel. Xiaomi's Leica manages to maintain the natural mood of the scene pretty well, with a slight cinematic touch, which I like. And like you said, noise-free.

text: Difference in mtf/lpmm between models within center is exactly zero, in some cases max 5%. Difference between edges is in average, 10%. Which means, 15 ultra is exactly the same in center, 10% better on edges, but only in case of aperture wide open. 15 Ultra is worse when aperture is used, from f/2.0 xiaomi 14 is better in whole image. And it's on slighty better level than Canon 7dmkIII used for comparison with medium level Canon lens with the same focal length and with Aperture closed to best possible value. Any statement about garbage - is ignorance and lie. Within 15ultra itself difference between edges is 10% (right side is better), within 14ultra difference is also 10% (also right side is better). No any difffeneces There is exactly zero chromatic aberration in 14 ultra - simply none. So direct lie here. There is even no need to use aperture. One of aperture applications is to remove chromatic abberations, but there is nothing to remove. Of course, 14 ultra has much better vignette from very definition. Aperture has a lot of applications, one of them is vignette removing. 15 Ultra must use software to do the same. All of this is without any real meaning. Using of aperture is changing whole photo at the base, which is visible from far even on miniatures, difference is in total different scale than few percent of details visible only on direct crops. Exactly the same in case of ultra wide lens. Visible even on miniatures from far away. There is a lot more applications where 15 Ultra is simply worthless. It is physically impossible to create the same quality photos. Especially in case of night photos, as aperture is changing light behavior at the very base. Many advanced photo technics are impossible or much harder to be used. A lot of ignorance is needed to not to understand that.

video_title: Xiaomi 15 Ultra FULL REVIEW - The New Camera King!? 👑
text: I described differences in quality above. Much better dynamic range alone creates a huge difference. Better sensitivity also allows to use faster shutters. Lenses on true similar quality level, equally fast and with equal resolution and aberration corrections are expensive, especially in case of FF where lenses quality is often far below what such sensor requires. Typical cheap lenses can not compare in any way. They are usually no similar fast, aberrations are much worse and aperture must be closed (to critical value like f/6), real resolution (lpmm, on raw without any sharpening) is directly lower especially on edges. Especially ultra wide (about 10mm) and wide ones (even from medium price range from Canon or Sigma) where DoF do not have any meaning and it's not wanted, are far worse. DoF in case of longer folcals is of course better on dslrs, but in practice what xiaomi can achieve in portret mode when used with proper skill can directly be better than for example Canon 85mm f/1.8. Which has terrible chromatic and others aberrations when fully opened - near not usable on full open. From f/2.4 or lower may be. Phone lenses are fully usable fully opened. Simply, pair of dedicated sensor with uncomparably better soft paired with dedicated fast fixed focal lens is far better corrected than random pairs with weak sensor and soft. I'm professional from 25years. I worked usually on hasselblads H series with medium format, also with big format, and with Canon 1d series, Nikons, Fiji, Olympus and others, Mostly studio and agency photos, but also lot of reporter work, city and nature (with a lot of ultra macro 16:1 type). Also I was reviewer of dozens of cameras and lenses, including very first dslrs introduced on marked, until last ones ever produced. I worked also a lot with analog including full darkroom manual work.

In short, in 'Photo' mode - you can see the focal length by tapping the 1x or 4.3x buttons (0.6x, 2x and 3x don't support this). This will let you toggle through 23mm 28mm and 35mm on the 1x lens, and 100mm, 200mm, and 400mm on the Ultra Telephoto. In 'Video' mode, tapping any lens selector (0.6x, 1x, 2x, 3x, 4.3x) will reveal the zoom wheel, which displays the focal length for each lens. This is true of each lens individually in the 'Pro' mode too. Less than ideal - but the info is there if you look for it.

video_title: iPhone 17 Review: No Asterisks!
text: I just went from an iPhone 14 Pro Max to an iPhone 17 base, and the only “complaint” I have is screen size. I lost a little bit of screen size, but for my stubby fingers, it’s actually become a blessing in disguise. The little bit small size means I can do more, on my phone, with only one hand. I have significant carpal tunnel in both hand, and reaching with my dominant thumb was starting to becoming painful from over stretching. With the slightly smaller screen, i don’t have to stretch as much. When I first went to look at upgrading, I wrote down a list of important things that I need vs. want, and the base iPhone 17 met all those features. If I want a bigger screen, I can mirror to my TV or get out my iPad. If I need more advanced photo editing, I can easily switch to my Mac Mini. I didn’t need all the bells and whistles that I needed when I chose the iPhone 14 Pro Max. I’m a few days into this new phone, and I haven’t found a feature on the base model that I miss that was exclusive to the Pro models. Maybe my 14 Pro Max was outdated enough to make me think that the 17 is amazing, but so far, I got a phone at half the price and I’m missing nothing!

video_title: iPhone 17 Review: No Asterisks!
text: 13 Pro Max users READ THIS I used the iphone 17 for around 24 hours and the phone is good phone do not get me wrong however coming from a 13 pro max these are the things i will say: Size: coming from the 13 pro max the size of the of the iphone 17 is noticeable but not that different. I will say if you have been using a smaller phone since forever then by all means this iphone is the best ever for you hands down. If you are a Pro Max user like myself you will definitely get jealous of a 17 Pro Max user once you hold their phone in your hand (this is from experience please trust me). Weight: I can tell you for a fact it was such a shock to hold this phone i will not lie to you it is so light compared to a 13 pro max like unimaginable and so unreal its crazy that a phone so light could be so powerful. Having felt the weight of the iphone 17 max as a 13 pro max user i will say it is much more comfortable to use and is lighter to use which is insane. The way i hold my iphone 13 pro max is i have my pinky at the bottom the phone, the finger close to my pinky and middle finger stretched at the edge and then the finger close to my thumb centered around the middle and then i use my thumb to type and its so much easier to do this on 17 pro max Battery: I have not used an iphone 17 pro max to test battery however from the people i know who has the 17 pro max they say the battery is the best ever and coming from a Pro Max line up i feel you will get extra confidence knowing that your phone is now set for the future in the long run and the upgrade will feel more worth it. Like i said i am speaking to 13 pro max users however the battery on the 17 was just as impressive i will say. Display: This is bright as hell dude like when i compared it to my 13 pro max it was absolutely noticeable and i love it. When i compared to how snappy it felt from 13 pro max you can tell the difference so i can’t even imagine how nice it is on the 17 pro max. If you are a small phone user you will not be disappointed with the display. Speakers: This little guy is really really loud i couldn’t begin to tell you how shocked i was when this thing played music it was sick deffo an upgrade. Dynamic Island: Coming from a 13 pro max we don’t get this luxury and for a few extra bucks i would say its worth having compared to what we get on the 13 pro max. Camera control & Action Button: i personally like these new features so getting this on either phone is just a plus dude especially coming from a phone that doesn’t have them. Selfie Camera and Camera: Wow lets just say game changer from 13 pro max its astounding definitely an upgrade which either one you go for Future Planning: i feel like the 12gb gives me a safer or confidence booster that if i don’t upgrade in the next year or 2 years that the phone will be equipped for any future software upgrade or AI upgrades so thats something u will need to take into consideration however if you are a casual user it won’t matter. All in all if you are 13 Pro Max user please do not make the mistake of going for the base 17. Yes you get a ton of upgrades but you will miss that screen and i feel like you just get added bonuses which is a bigger screen, better battery with a brand chip to help with performance, comfort and much better cameras which is why i am returning it. SMALL PHONE USERS - Best ever phone you can get a hold of right now.

One of the features you mentioned, "PWM dimming", is the default for nearly every OLED screen on the market -- this isn't a noteworthy feature. So you didn't even understand what the tech was and yet touted it as something Apple "really nailed"? My Pixel has 256GB, 48 MP ultrawide, 120Hz, always-on screen, PWM dimming, 3,300 nits, scratch resisitant display, thinner borders, 6.3" OLED, and a 48 MP selfie camera. This is what most top-tier phone specs are in 2025. Apple is just playing keep-up.

video_title: iPhone 17 Review: No Asterisks!
text: The Air taught me that Pro phones aren't even for Pros. Conversations around the Air are often "the camera sucks, the speaker sucks, the battery is bad." As if 'professional' people are watching movies on their phones or filming movies on them. I can't imagine wanting to watch a movie on a phone instead of a TV with quality speakers. The Pro phone is the real compromise phone. It's the phone you get if you can't use a TV, if you can't listen to your music on good speakers. It's the phone you use if you can't charge your phone daily. I love that the new Apple lineup is really forcing people to think about their lifestyle choices. And what they really need out of a phone. I used to always get the Pro because I needed Always on Display. Now I can get that in the Air, and I'm happy about it.

I've turned those features off on my iphone and it has made a huge difference. Another tip I found is if you're carrier supports it, whenever you are connected to wifi, you should enable wifi calling and turn on airplane mode. That way your iphone doesn't waste battery always trying to get a cell signal when it can just use wifi calling with airplane mode. Airplane mode is also good because it shuts of bluetooth connection save battery too! I think these features would be worth mentioning in your future videos!

text: Actually, the relationship could just as easily run the other way. If dbrand’s Pro Max accessories outsell the rest, that doesn’t only mean Pro Max owners are more accessory-prone—it can also be read as indirect evidence that there are simply more Pro Max owners in the first place. Think about it: if the Pro Max had a relatively small user base, then even if every single owner bought a dbrand skin or case, the raw sales numbers would still struggle to beat out accessories for models with larger user bases. For dbrand’s Pro Max sales to dominate, the pool of potential buyers has to be pretty big to begin with. It’s the reverse of your battery-pack example: yes, Air buyers might be more likely to buy one, but if that group were tiny compared to Pro Max buyers, their accessory sales would never overtake. So rather than being a confounder, accessory sales can serve as a rough proxy for the underlying sales success of the phone itself.
